3.happening: Checking whether head is an empty list.
3.why: An empty list has nothing to reverse — we detect this first to avoid entering the loop needlessly.

4.happening: Assigning result = [] for the empty-input path.
4.why: We need a value to return; reversing an empty list gives an empty list.

5.why: No reversal is needed; the answer is trivially empty.
5.invariant: Correct result for an empty input.

6.happening: Building head_links = {head_links} — the initial sequential link array.
6.why: The renderer needs explicit next-pointers to draw arrows. We build them once here so they can be mutated step by step as we reverse the list.

7.why: After reversal, the original head node becomes the tail — its 'next' pointer must point to None (no next node).
7.invariant: previous = None; no arrows flipped yet.

8.happening: Setting current = 0 — the index of the head node.
8.why: We begin at the first node and walk forward through every node exactly once.

9.happening: Resetting changed_links = [] before entering the loop.
9.why: changed_links records which arrow to flip each step; starting fresh ensures no stale arrow from a previous run.

10.happening: current ({current}) < {len(head)} — still more nodes to reverse. Entering the loop body.
10.why: We visit every node exactly once to flip its arrow.
10.invariant: All nodes before index {current} have had their arrows reversed.

10.happening: current ({current}) >= {len(head)} — all nodes processed. Exiting the loop.
10.why: Every arrow now points backward; previous is the new head.
10.invariant: All arrows reversed; previous = {previous} is the new head index.

11.why: Once we flip current's arrow to point backward, we'd lose track of the next node without this save.
11.invariant: nxt safely holds the index of the next node to process.

12.happening: Updating head_links[{current}] to point at {previous} — recording the reversed link.
12.why: head_links must always reflect the true current link state so the renderer shows the right arrows each step.

13.happening: Flipping node {current}'s arrow to point at previous ({previous}) — the core reversal step!
13.why: This is the algorithm's entire job: redirect the forward arrow into a backward arrow. The renderer animates this transition.
13.invariant: Node {current} now points at previous instead of forward.

14.why: On the next iteration, the following node must point back to the current node.
14.invariant: previous always trails one step behind current.

15.why: We advance to the next unprocessed node using the saved nxt value.
15.invariant: current has moved forward; previous is one step behind it.

16.why: Materialises the reversed value sequence for the result readout — the visual answer.
16.invariant: result is the complete reversed list.

17.why: All arrows have been flipped; the reversal is done.
17.invariant: The linked list is fully reversed; result holds the answer.

init.happening: Setting up head_links, previous, current, and changed_links.
init.why: head_links gives the renderer the initial forward arrows; previous, current, and changed_links drive the reversal loop.

check.why: An empty input needs no work.

update.happening: Updating state — saving nxt, flipping head_links, recording changed_links, or advancing previous.
update.why: Each update makes the reversal one arrow closer to complete.
update.invariant: All nodes before current are reversed.

loop.why: Deciding whether there are more arrows to flip.
loop.invariant: All nodes before current index have been reversed.

move.happening: Advancing current to the next node.
move.why: Moving forward through the original list order.
move.invariant: previous is exactly one step behind current.

return.invariant: All arrows point backward; the answer is ready.
